claims.claim: We investigate whether irrelevant, low-information prompt material imposes a measurable cost on local LLM inference—a phenomenon we term the "garbage token tax." We define three operational dimensions: compute tax (additional prompt-prefill wall time), memory tax (additional context/KV allocation), and quality tax (loss of task accuracy).

evidence_refs.quote: # Garbage Token Tax: Measuring the Latency and Context-Budget Cost of Irrelevant Prompt Material in Local LLM Inference

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Using a Q4_K_M quantized Phi-4-mini-instruct model on an NVIDIA GB10 GPU via llama.cpp CUDA, we benchmark prompt-prefill latency at 64, 512, and 2,048 prompt tokens and run a small QA smoke test (5 arithmetic prompts per condition) with clean versus garbage-prefixed prompts.

evidence_refs.quote: # Garbage Token Tax: Measuring the Latency and Context-Budget Cost of Irrelevant Prompt Material in Local LLM Inference

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We find a clear compute tax: marginal prefill cost ranges from approximately 0.16 ms/token (64→512 tokens) to 0.27 ms/token (512→2,048 tokens), and end-to-end QA latency increases by approximately 0.35 s when prepending ~2k garbage tokens.

evidence_refs.quote: # Garbage Token Tax: Measuring the Latency and Context-Budget Cost of Irrelevant Prompt Material in Local LLM Inference

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We observe memory tax in the form of CUDA context allocation scaling from 256 MiB (context 2,048) to 512 MiB (context 4,096).

evidence_refs.quote: # Garbage Token Tax: Measuring the Latency and Context-Budget Cost of Irrelevant Prompt Material in Local LLM Inference

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: However, we do **not** find evidence of a quality tax on this task: garbage-prefixed conditions matched or exceeded clean-condition accuracy, though the sample is too small to support any conclusion about quality effects in either direction.

evidence_refs.quote: # Garbage Token Tax: Measuring the Latency and Context-Budget Cost of Irrelevant Prompt Material in Local LLM Inference

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We conclude that the garbage token tax is a viable and measurable systems-level phenomenon on the latency and context-budget axes, but that quality degradation claims require substantially stronger evidence than this run provides.
